(1 comment)

Thanks for the work.
I would suggest to try the change based on https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/54469. The file liblistreapi_pcc.c shows how JSON parser works to search/add/remove an entry of HSM. If we add more entry (e.g., "hotpool") in the future, these code should work without change. That's the major reason we design the content of 'trusted.pin' a YAML or JSON string.

Patch Set 5:

(1 comment)

I agree that YAML is cleaner and neater than JSON. But the YAML parser in C is really most of a reader and parser. It is hard to navigate and modify the object tree as freely as JSON-C, whether in DOM or SAX model.

Patch Set 5:

(1 comment)

> Patch Set 5:
> 
> (1 comment)
> 
> I agree that YAML is cleaner and neater than JSON. But the YAML parser in C is really most of a reader and parser. It is hard to navigate and modify the object tree as freely as JSON-C, whether in DOM or SAX model.

Its not hard. I just did it with this patch. The real issue is what happens if we need more than 64K of space. The xattr approach would need to be replaced with Netlink since it can handle very large messages. Note ioctl also have size limitations. We have a Netlink libyaml solution that just works with a few line changes in user land. Can't do that with libjson :-(

Patch Set 5:

> The real issue is what happens if we need more than 64K of space.

I don't think that is a real issue at all. This xattr has a few bytes in it today, to indicate which layout components should be preserved by PCC-RO and which ones can be released under cache pressure. It might have a few times this in the future (ie. a few dozen bytes) if there are multiple components and they will be pinned for PCC and flash. 

It can never get to 64KB since the layout xattr itself is limited to 64 KB and the pin xattr is always just a subset of this. 

Honestly, I would prefer to just have sprintf() and sscanf() (or similar) to deal with the 2-3 fields in this xattr rather than invoking complex libraries to do a simple job...

Patch Set 5:

> Patch Set 5:
> 
> > The real issue is what happens if we need more than 64K of space.
> 
> I don't think that is a real issue at all. This xattr has a few bytes in it today, to indicate which layout components should be preserved by PCC-RO and which ones can be released under cache pressure. It might have a few times this in the future (ie. a few dozen bytes) if there are multiple components and they will be pinned for PCC and flash. 
> 
> It can never get to 64KB since the layout xattr itself is limited to 64 KB and the pin xattr is always just a subset of this. 
> 
> Honestly, I would prefer to just have sprintf() and sscanf() (or similar) to deal with the 2-3 fields in this xattr rather than invoking complex libraries to do a simple job...

Well, I remember there used to be a discussion about the format of this attribute. There are 3 options at that time:
- user defined format, something like "hsm:2,3,4". Most simple and easy to be parsed by string functions, but not flexible.
- YAML, something like "hsm: [2, 3, 4]". It can be parsed by YAML lib, but hard to change. Actually at that time we use cYAML functions to parse and change it, plus some hacking.
- JSON, something like '{"hsm": [2, 3, 4]}'. It can be parsed and modified by JSON lib.
Lastly JSON format won.

Patch Set 5:

It could be JSON *format* while being read/written by regular C string functions, if that is less complex than using the library to do so?

Patch Set 5:

> Patch Set 5:
> 
> It could be JSON *format* while being read/written by regular C string functions, if that is less complex than using the library to do so?

It is not hard if we know/restrict all the possible items in the string. Today the string has only hsm and an array of numbers, like '{"hsm": [2, 3, 4]}', or YAML format 'hsm: [2, 3, 4]'. But if any item is added into the string in the future, we need to double check all the parse/dump functions and possibly modify them. We cannot have both simple parse/dump functions and flexible content.

Patch Set 5:

> Patch Set 5:
> 
> > Patch Set 5:
> > 
> > It could be JSON *format* while being read/written by regular C string functions, if that is less complex than using the library to do so?
> 
> It is not hard if we know/restrict all the possible items in the string. Today the string has only hsm and an array of numbers, like '{"hsm": [2, 3, 4]}', or YAML format 'hsm: [2, 3, 4]'. But if any item is added into the string in the future, we need to double check all the parse/dump functions and possibly modify them. We cannot have both simple parse/dump functions and flexible content.

Actually it is a user-defined format happening to be compatible to JSON/YAML. We must define every word and punctuation in the string.

Patch Set 5:

I'm have no issue with just string modification. I hate the idea of adding a new library for one specific use case. Especially since libyaml has wide adoption.

Patch Set 5:

>

I agree with a user-defined format happening to be compatible to YAML to make this attribute cleaner.
As I know JSON libaray is still in use in other places. At least in lpcc_purge to generate status report. Perhaps we can link json libraray only to individual programs instead of liblustreapi libaray.

parents.subject: LU-10499 pcc: tunable GID to pin/unpin files

commit.subject: LU-10499 utils: use libyaml for pin xattr handling
commit.message:
LU-10499 utils: use libyaml for pin xattr handling

Change the format of trusted.pin xattr to a YAML flow sequence.
This allows us to store far more data in the xattr

trusted.pin="hsm: [ 2, 50, 100 ]"

To use this new format move from using cYAML which doesn't support
flow sequence to libyaml. The move to libyaml enables using the
Netlink API as well if we ever need far more data.

Update the libyaml configure test to PKG_CHECK_MODULES. This gives
us the proper CFLAGS and LIBS flags for libyaml.

Test-Parameters: trivial testlist=sanity-pcc
Change-Id: I5f264d425ef047350560244cced7f1794c0c15f8
Signed-off-by: James Simmons <jsimmons@infradead.org>
